topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, bright and unwelcome. 'Sarah_Writes' had just updated her status, a casual mention of a local coffee shop they both frequented. My stomach did a tight, sickening flip. I stared at the screen, the blue light reflecting off my suddenly clammy hands. It was impossible. I’d always pictured her as some distant, idealized figure, a voice across state lines. Now, the reality was staring me down, tangible and infuriatingly close. I quickly minimized the chat window, the sound of my own shallow breathing suddenly loud in the quiet apartment. I scrubbed at my face, a frantic, pointless gesture. How could I possibly pretend I didn't know her face, or the way she laughed over text? The sheer awkwardness of the situation made my throat constrict.

[story 2]

I kept glancing at my phone, a nervous tic I hadn't realized I possessed. The profile picture, usually a carefully curated landscape, was now a grainy photo taken near the old municipal library. My jaw tightened, and I instinctively pulled my shoulders up around my ears, a defensive posture. It was just a coincidence, surely. A shared interest, a geographical overlap. But the thought that this person, the one whose witty banter I relied on for a certain amount of daily comfort, was just a few blocks away, felt like a sudden, heavy weight settling on my chest. I started pacing the small living room, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the hardwood floor. I needed an escape, a way to delete the memory of the connection, but the app remained open, a beacon of unwelcome proximity.

[story 3]

When the message arrived—a simple, "Running into you at the park later?"—I felt the blood drain from my face. It was too direct, too real. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. My mind raced, constructing elaborate scenarios where this was a mistake, a glitch in the matrix, something utterly untrue. I looked around my room, cataloging every object, trying to anchor myself to something solid. The realization that this online confidante shared my zip code sent a wave of intense discomfort washing over me. I couldn't meet her eyes, couldn't offer a casual greeting. I just wanted the screen to go dark, to erase the undeniable evidence that my carefully constructed digital bubble had just burst open.
